locations.name: General Sherman Tree
locations.description: The General Sherman Tree is the world's largest tree by volume, estimated at 52,500 cubic feet (1,487 m³). It stands 274.9 feet tall with a base circumference of 102.6 feet. Growing in the Giant Forest of Sequoia National Park, it is estimated to be around 2,200 years old. The tree is a living monument to the immense scale life can achieve given time and the right conditions.
locations.location: Giant Forest, Sequoia National Park, Tulare County

locations.name: General Grant Tree
locations.description: Known as the 'Nation's Christmas Tree,' the General Grant Tree is the world's second largest tree by volume at approximately 46,608 cubic feet. Standing 267.4 feet tall, it was designated a National Shrine in 1956 — the only living tree with that honor — as a memorial to American soldiers who died in war. Congress designated it in 1926 to serve as the nation's Christmas tree.
locations.location: Grant Grove, Kings Canyon National Park, Fresno County

locations.name: Hyperion (Tallest Known Tree)
locations.description: Hyperion is the world's tallest known living tree, a coast redwood measuring 380.3 feet (115.9 m) tall — taller than a 35-story building. It was discovered in 2006 by naturalists Chris Atkins and Michael Taylor in a remote area of Redwood National Park. The NPS keeps its exact location secret to protect it from overvisitation and trampling of its root zone. These coordinates are approximate.
locations.location: Redwood National Park, Del Norte / Humboldt Counties (approximate location)

locations.name: Chandelier Drive-Thru Tree
locations.description: The Chandelier Drive-Thru Tree is a 315-foot coast redwood with a 6×9 foot tunnel carved through its base in the 1930s, wide enough for a car to pass through. It stands in Drive-Thru Tree Park in Leggett, a privately operated roadside attraction on the old Highway 1. Despite the tunnel, the tree remains very much alive. It is one of only three remaining drive-thru trees in California.
locations.location: Drive-Thru Tree Park, Leggett, Mendocino County

locations.name: Methuselah
locations.description: Methuselah is a Great Basin bristlecone pine in the Ancient Bristlecone Pine Forest of the White Mountains, long believed to be the oldest known non-clonal organism on Earth at roughly 4,855 years old. For decades it held the verified record for oldest individual tree. Its exact location within Schulman Grove is kept secret by the Forest Service to protect it. These coordinates point to the grove area.
locations.location: Schulman Grove, Inyo National Forest, White Mountains

locations.name: Colonel Armstrong Tree
locations.description: The Colonel Armstrong Tree is a coast redwood estimated to be over 1,400 years old, standing 308 feet tall and 14.6 feet in diameter. It is the centerpiece of Armstrong Redwoods State Natural Reserve near Guerneville in Sonoma County. The grove was protected in the 1870s by lumberman Colonel James Armstrong, who recognized its value and set it aside — one of the earliest private conservation efforts for redwoods.
locations.location: Armstrong Redwoods State Natural Reserve, Guerneville, Sonoma County

locations.name: The President Tree
locations.description: The President is the third-largest tree in the world by volume, a giant sequoia in Sequoia National Park estimated at 45,148 cubic feet. Standing 247 feet tall with a 93-foot circumference at its base, it is around 3,200 years old — making it one of the oldest giant sequoias. In 2012, National Geographic conducted a landmark photogrammetric study, producing the first complete image of the enormous tree.
locations.location: Congress Trail area, Sequoia National Park, Tulare County

locations.name: Grizzly Giant
locations.description: The Grizzly Giant is the oldest and largest sequoia in Yosemite's Mariposa Grove, estimated to be between 1,900 and 2,700 years old. It stands 209 feet tall with a massive base circumference of 96 feet. Its first large branch alone is larger than most Eastern forest trees. Discovered in 1849, it became one of the first giant sequoias to gain national attention and helped inspire early conservation legislation.
locations.location: Mariposa Grove, Yosemite National Park, Mariposa County

locations.name: Pioneer Cabin Tree (fallen 2017)
locations.description: The Pioneer Cabin Tree was a giant sequoia in Calaveras Big Trees State Park with a tunnel carved through its base in the 1880s for tourist stagecoaches. For over a century it was a beloved California icon. On January 8, 2017, after a powerful atmospheric river storm saturated the soil, the tree fell — ending its 1,000+ year life. The stump and fallen log remain at the site as a natural memorial.
locations.location: Calaveras Big Trees State Park, Calaveras County

locations.name: Sentinel Tree
locations.description: The Sentinel Tree is a massive ponderosa pine that stands as a solitary landmark on the floor of Yosemite Valley near Yosemite Village. Its commanding silhouette against El Capitan and Half Dome has made it one of the most photographed trees in the park. Valley ponderosa pines can live 300–600 years; the Sentinel is an example of the old-growth forest that once filled the valley floor before early visitor-era landscape management.
locations.location: Yosemite Valley, Yosemite National Park, Mariposa County

locations.name: Berkeley Blue Gum Forest
locations.description: Strawberry Canyon above UC Berkeley is home to a dramatic blue gum eucalyptus forest planted in the early 1900s when eucalyptus was promoted as a fast-growing timber crop. The trees grew to 200+ feet, creating a cathedral-like grove beloved by Bay Area hikers. The grove is ecologically controversial — blue gums are non-native and displace native habitat — but culturally significant and visually stunning, especially in morning fog.
locations.location: Strawberry Canyon, UC Berkeley Hills, Alameda County

locations.name: Moreton Bay Fig (Golden Gate Park)
locations.description: This Moreton Bay fig (Ficus macrophylla) near the Music Concourse in Golden Gate Park has a canopy spread exceeding 150 feet — so wide that its branches touch the ground and re-root. Planted in the 1800s, it is one of San Francisco's most spectacular trees. The buttressed root system creates a surreal landscape that invites exploration. It is a designated San Francisco landmark.

locations.name: Moreton Bay Fig (Santa Barbara Amtrak)
locations.description: This enormous Moreton Bay fig next to the Santa Barbara Amtrak station is one of the largest trees in North America by canopy spread, with branches extending nearly 170 feet across. Planted in 1877 from an Australian seedling, it now stands 80 feet tall with a circumference of over 38 feet. It has been a California State Historic Landmark since 1970. Train passengers disembark directly beneath its shade.
locations.location: Santa Barbara Amtrak Station, Chapala & Mason, Santa Barbara

locations.name: Pechanga Great Oak
locations.description: The Pechanga Great Oak is a coast live oak (Quercus agrifolia) estimated to be over 1,000 years old, making it one of the oldest oaks in North America. It stands on the Pechanga Band of Luiseño Indians' reservation near Temecula. The tribe considers the tree sacred and has long protected it. When a casino was proposed nearby in the 1990s, the tribe specifically redesigned the project to preserve the oak's root zone.
locations.location: Pechanga Reservation, Temecula area, Riverside County

locations.name: Deep Creek Hot Springs
locations.description: Deep Creek Hot Springs are among Southern California's most popular backcountry hot springs, with several pools of varying temperature (100–108°F) along the banks of Deep Creek. The hike in is 3–6 miles depending on the trailhead. Located in San Bernardino National Forest, the springs are clothing-optional and draw a diverse crowd. Spring and fall offer the best conditions; flash floods are a hazard in the canyon during storms.
locations.location: San Bernardino National Forest, San Bernardino County

locations.name: Travertine Hot Springs
locations.description: Travertine Hot Springs, just south of Bridgeport, offers stunning views of the Eastern Sierra and multiple tiered pools formed by calcium carbonate deposits that give the springs their name. Temperatures range from a gentle 80°F in upper pools to a scalding 115°F near the source — choose your pool carefully. Managed by the BLM, the site is free and open year-round. The dramatic white travertine formations and Sierra backdrop make this one of California's most photogenic springs.
locations.location: BLM land south of Bridgeport, Mono County

locations.name: Buckeye Hot Springs
locations.description: Buckeye Hot Springs sits in a canyon along Buckeye Creek in the Toiyabe National Forest, with a striking waterfall of hot mineral water cascading into a cool creek below. Soakers can mix hot and cold to find their perfect temperature. The drive in via a dirt road requires high clearance in wet conditions. At roughly 110°F at the source, it's best enjoyed in the pool just above creek level. A true Eastern Sierra gem.
locations.location: Toiyabe National Forest, near Bridgeport, Mono County

locations.name: Wild Willy's Hot Springs
locations.description: Wild Willy's Hot Springs (also called Crowley Hot Springs) are a cluster of shallow soaking pools on BLM land east of Mammoth Lakes with panoramic views of the Eastern Sierra. A short boardwalk leads from the parking area across the meadow to the pools, which range from 100–107°F. Open year-round, though winter access requires a snowmobile or skis. Named for a local character, the site is free and very accessible compared to other Eastern Sierra springs.
locations.location: BLM land east of Mammoth Lakes, Mono County

locations.name: Saline Valley Warm Springs
locations.description: Saline Valley Warm Springs are an oasis in the remote Saline Valley within Death Valley National Park. Three developed pools sit in a palm-studded desert setting at around 105°F. Reaching them requires 4WD or high-clearance vehicle on 50+ miles of rough dirt road — plan for a multi-day trip. A caretaker community maintains the site, which has an established camping area with outhouses. Clothing is optional. This is one of California's most remote and spectacular soaking destinations.
locations.location: Death Valley National Park backcountry, Inyo County

locations.name: Hot Creek Geological Site
locations.description: Hot Creek Geological Site near Mammoth Lakes is a spectacular geothermal area where boiling water erupts from the creek bed, creating vivid blue-green pools surrounded by steam vents. Soaking is prohibited since 2006 due to unpredictable eruptions that have caused serious scalding injuries. However, the site remains open for viewing from designated areas on the canyon rim, and is a fascinating window into the Mammoth Lakes volcanic field. Swimming is not permitted.
locations.location: Mammoth Lakes area, Mono County (soaking prohibited)

locations.name: Keough's Hot Ditch
locations.description: Keough's Hot Ditch is a free, informal soaking spot just south of Bishop in the Owens Valley, where hot spring water flows through a concrete irrigation ditch before cooling in a series of informal pools. Temperatures near the source reach 110°F and cool progressively downstream. The site sits on BLM land with views of the Sierra Nevada and the White Mountains. Primitive camping is available nearby. A local favorite for its no-frills accessibility.
locations.location: BLM land south of Bishop, Owens Valley, Inyo County

locations.name: Gaviota Hot Springs
locations.description: Gaviota Hot Springs are a pair of small, lithium-rich mineral pools tucked into a canyon within Gaviota State Park along the Santa Barbara coast. A 1-mile round-trip hike leads up a shaded canyon to the springs, which emerge at around 104°F. The pools are small (2–3 people each) and can get crowded on weekends. Park entry fee applies. A wonderful combination of coast and mountain: the Pacific Ocean is visible from higher up the trail.
locations.location: Gaviota State Park, Santa Barbara County

locations.name: Sespe Hot Springs
locations.description: Sespe Hot Springs are among the most remote hot springs in California, requiring a 16–20 mile round-trip hike through the Sespe Wilderness in Los Padres National Forest. The reward is a series of pools ranging from 95–110°F in a dramatic sandstone canyon, with California condors frequently overhead (the nearby Sespe Condor Sanctuary is critical habitat). Plan for an overnight backpack. Permits may be required. This is a true wilderness experience.
locations.location: Sespe Wilderness, Los Padres National Forest, Ventura County

locations.name: Remington Hot Springs
locations.description: Remington Hot Springs sit on the banks of the Kern River in a gorgeous river canyon setting, with two main pools carved into the rocks right at river's edge. Hot spring water seeps through the rocks at around 104°F and mixes with cool river water for adjustable soaking. BLM-managed, free, and open year-round. The site is accessed via a short but steep trail from the road. The Kern River's emerald water and surrounding canyon walls make this spot exceptionally scenic.
locations.location: Kern River Canyon, BLM land, Kern County

locations.name: Miracle Hot Springs
locations.description: Miracle Hot Springs are located just downstream from Remington Hot Springs along the Kern River, offering a similar riverside soaking experience in a slightly more developed setting. Multiple pools of varying temperatures (95–105°F) line the rocky banks of the Kern River. The site was historically a resort and retains some concrete infrastructure. BLM-managed and free to access, it's a popular destination for paddlers who stop here during Kern River kayaking trips.
locations.location: Kern River Canyon, BLM land, Kern County

locations.name: Mono Hot Springs
locations.description: Mono Hot Springs is a small resort community in the Sierra Nevada of Fresno County, perched at 6,500 feet elevation along the South Fork San Joaquin River. The natural hot springs feed a historic resort with private tubs, a bathhouse, and a small general store. Wild soakers will find free pools in the river below the resort. The area sits near Florence Lake and Vermilion Valley Resort at the gateway to the John Muir Wilderness. The drive in via Kaiser Pass Road is scenic but winding.

locations.name: Grover Hot Springs
locations.description: Grover Hot Springs State Park near Markleeville is a classic California hot springs experience: a developed pool maintained at 102–104°F, set in a high mountain meadow surrounded by the Sierra Nevada at 5,900 feet. The park also has a cold pool, campgrounds, and trails into the Toiyabe National Forest. The mineral water is naturally tinged green. Open year-round (conditions permitting), it's a beloved destination for families and winter soakers looking for a structured hot springs outing.
locations.location: Grover Hot Springs State Park, Markleeville, Alpine County

locations.name: Vichy Springs Resort
locations.description: Vichy Springs Resort in Ukiah is California's only naturally carbonated warm mineral springs, operating continuously since 1854. The naturally effervescent 90°F water—sometimes called 'champagne baths'—was visited by Mark Twain, Jack London, Ulysses Grant, and Theodore Roosevelt. The resort offers day use and overnight accommodations. The carbon dioxide-saturated mineral water is said to have therapeutic properties. It is a California Historical Landmark and one of the state's most historically significant spas.
locations.location: Vichy Springs Resort, Ukiah, Mendocino County

locations.name: Old Faithful Geyser of California
locations.description: The Old Faithful Geyser of California in Calistoga is one of only three 'Old Faithful' geysers in the world that erupts on a somewhat regular schedule, shooting a column of 350°F water 60–100 feet into the air roughly every 30 minutes. Located in the Napa Valley geothermal zone, Calistoga itself is renowned for its mud baths and hot springs resorts fed by the same volcanic system. The geyser is a privately operated attraction with an admission fee.

locations.name: Benton Hot Springs
locations.description: Benton Hot Springs is a small historic mining town turned hot springs resort in the remote Mono County high desert, sitting at 5,600 feet between the Sierra Nevada and the White Mountains. The resort offers private outdoor tubs filled with geothermal water ranging from 100–110°F, alongside rustic accommodations including a historic inn and camping. The remote setting, dark skies, and panoramic desert-mountain views make it exceptional for stargazing. Reservations required.

locations.name: Tecopa Hot Springs
locations.description: Tecopa Hot Springs in the Mojave Desert near Death Valley is a small desert community built around natural lithium-rich mineral springs that reach 108°F. The Inyo County parks department operates free outdoor soaking pools segregated by sex, open 24 hours. Numerous private campgrounds and motels cater to the snowbird crowd that descends each winter. The springs have long been rumored to ease arthritis and other ailments. A distinctly quirky and affordable desert soaking destination.

locations.name: Esalen Institute Hot Springs
locations.description: The Esalen Institute hot springs are cliffside baths perched dramatically above the Pacific Ocean at Big Sur, fed by natural hot springs at 104–108°F. Esalen is a world-renowned center for humanistic alternative education, but its famous baths are accessible to the general public only from 1–3 AM for a modest fee — reservations required and book weeks in advance. Clothing is optional. The experience — soaking in mineral water with waves crashing below and stars above — is one of California's most singular.
locations.location: Esalen Institute, Big Sur, Monterey County (public access 1–3 AM only, reservations required)

locations.name: Wister Mud Pot
locations.description: Wister Mud Pot is a natural hot spring catalogued in the USGS Water Supply Paper 338, a comprehensive survey of California geothermal springs. Water temperature reaches 100°F. Located in the Salton Sea area, Imperial County area near the NILAND 7.5 quadrangle. Verify current access, land status, and conditions before visiting — some sites are on private land or require permits.
